nanoparticles,
thus
biomaterials
possessing
both
adhesive
and

protective
characteristics
seem to
be
desirable
for
oral
peptide

delivery [16]. To achieve
such an aim, different polymers have been

utilized
to
formulate
biodegradable
nanoparticles,
among
which

poly(D,L-lactide-co-glycolide)
(PLGA)
and
poly(lactic
acid)
(PLA)
approved biocompatible and biodegradable polymers have been exten-
sively studied [17]. Despite somewhat achievements, PLGA copolymers
retain problems associated with their physicochemical properties and

methods of
fabrication. For example, hydrophilic macromolecules, such
as polypeptides cannot readily diffuse through hydrophobic particles of
PLGA or PLA; drug loading and device fabrication using PLA or PLGA

often requires
toxic organic
solvents, and the solid dosage form may

induce
tissue
irritation.
Intriguingly,
uptake
of
hydrophobic
nano-
particles seems to be higher than hydrophilic nanoparticles. Thus, an
optimal hydrophobic-hydrophilic balance is required for the controlled

delivery
of
drugs. Further,
hydrophilic
biodegradable materials
are

required
for
incorporation
of water
soluble
polypeptide
drugs
in

solution,
in
particular when
controlled
delivery
of
protein/peptide

drugs
is desired.
In the
case
of
linear polyesters
consisting
of PLA
or PLGA this is partially achieved by copolymerization or adjustment

of molecular weight. However,
in many cases
the release of peptides

and
proteins
from linear
polyesters
is
not
satisfactorily
controlled,
leading to undesired discontinuous or polyphasic release patterns [18].
To overcome these problems, two major modifications of the polymers

have been proposed, namely:
(a)
increasing the hydrophilicity of
the

polymers, and (b) accelerating the degradation rate of
linear polyesters
by branching PLA or PLGA. An example for the first approach is the

linear ‘ABA’
triblock copolymers consisting of
the hydrophobic PLGA

and central hydrophilic poly ethylene glycol
(PEG) as A and B blocks,
respectively. Such architecture appears to provide a more rapid swelling

characteristics and accelerated degradation rates
[19,20]. As
for
the

second
concept,
star-branched
polyesters,
brush like
grafted PLGA,

and PLGAs with low molecular multifunctional
alcohols have
been
described [21–23].

In the
current
investigation,
various PLGA based nanostructures

possessing
linear
and
star-branched
architecture were
synthesized.

To
pursue
the
impact
of
structural
organization
of
these
nano-

structures
for
peptide/protein
delivery,
in
which
characterization

and
degradation of
linear PEG-PLGA,
star-branched b-cyclodextrin-

PLGA
and
glucose-PLGA
(Glu-PLGA)
copolymers
(b-CD-PLGA),
are studied.
